notes: Extracted in a single pass. Chapter is dense with scripture quotations (attributed_quote), Millerite chronology claims (date), and interpretive/theological framing of the parable of the ten virgins and Old Testament types. Footnote markers (e.g., (645)) present in source were trimmed from quote fields as citation apparatus, not prose content.

claims.quote: When the time passed at which the Lord’s coming was first expected,—in the spring of 1844,—those who had looked in faith for His appearing were for a season involved in doubt and uncertainty.
claims.claim_text: Millerite Adventists first expected Christ's second coming to occur in the spring of 1844, and experienced doubt and uncertainty when that time passed without His appearing.

claims.quote: Many continued to search the Scriptures, examining anew the evidences of their faith, and carefully studying the prophecies to obtain further light.
claims.claim_text: After the spring 1844 disappointment, many Adventist believers continued to study Scripture and prophecy to seek further understanding.

claims.quote: The special blessing of the Lord, both in the conversion of sinners and the revival of spiritual life among Christians, had testified that the message was of Heaven.
claims.claim_text: The conversions and spiritual revival accompanying the Advent message testified that the message came from Heaven.

claims.quote: though the believers could not explain their disappointment, they felt assured that God had led them in their past experience.
claims.claim_text: Despite being unable to explain their disappointment, Adventist believers felt assured that God had led them in their past experience.

claims.quote: Interwoven with prophecies which they had regarded as applying to the time of the second advent, was instruction specially adapted to their state of uncertainty and suspense, and encouraging them to wait patiently in the faith that what was now dark to their understanding would in due time be made plain.
claims.claim_text: Scripture passages surrounding the second-advent prophecies were providentially adapted to the Adventists' state of uncertainty, encouraging them to wait patiently.
claims.extractor_note: Interpretive/providential framing rather than a discrete checkable fact.

claims.quote: “I will stand upon my watch, and set me upon the tower, and will watch to see what He will say unto me, and what I shall answer when I am reproved. And the Lord answered me, and said, Write the vision, and make it plain upon tables, that he may run that readeth it. For the vision is yet for an appointed time, but at the end it shall speak, and not lie: though it tarry, wait for it; because it will surely come, it will not tarry. Behold, his soul which is lifted up is not upright in him: but the just shall live by his faith.”
claims.claim_text: Habakkuk 2:1-4 contains this wording as quoted here (attributed to the prophet Habakkuk).

claims.quote: As early as 1842, the direction given in this prophecy, to “write the vision, and make it plain upon tables, that he may run that readeth it,” had suggested to Charles Fitch the preparation of a prophetic chart to illustrate the visions of Daniel and the Revelation.
claims.claim_text: As early as 1842, Charles Fitch prepared a prophetic chart illustrating the visions of Daniel and Revelation, inspired by Habakkuk 2:2.

claims.quote: The publication of this chart was regarded as a fulfilment of the command given by Habakkuk.
claims.claim_text: Millerites regarded the publication of Charles Fitch's prophetic chart as a fulfilment of Habakkuk's command to "write the vision."

claims.quote: “The word of the Lord came unto me, saying, Son of man, what is that proverb that ye have in the land of Israel, saying, The days are prolonged, and every vision faileth? Tell them therefore, Thus saith the Lord God, ... The days are at hand, and the effect of every vision.... I will speak, and the word that I shall speak shall come to pass; it shall be no more prolonged.” “They of the house of Israel say, The vision that he seeth is for many days to come, and he prophesieth of the times that are far off. Therefore say unto them, Thus saith the Lord God: There shall none of My words be prolonged any more, but the word which I have spoken shall be done.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Ezekiel (chapter 12) contains this text, quoted here as attributed to the prophet Ezekiel.
claims.extractor_note: Quotation includes original ellipses present in the 1911 source.

claims.quote: The waiting ones rejoiced, believing that He who knows the end from the beginning had looked down through the ages, and foreseeing their disappointment, had given them words of courage and hope.
claims.claim_text: God, foreseeing the Adventists' disappointment, had given them scriptural words of courage and hope in advance.

claims.quote: The parable of the ten virgins of Matthew 25 also illustrates the experience of the Adventist people.
claims.claim_text: The parable of the ten virgins in Matthew 25 is a prophetic illustration of the experience of the Adventist people.

claims.quote: In Matthew 24, in answer to the question of His disciples concerning the sign of His coming and of the end of the world, Christ had pointed out some of the most important events in the history of the world and of the church from His first to His second advent; namely, the destruction of Jerusalem, the great tribulation of the church under the pagan and papal persecutions, the darkening of the sun and moon, and the falling of the stars.
claims.claim_text: Matthew 24 records Christ identifying the destruction of Jerusalem, tribulation under pagan and papal persecution, the darkening of the sun and moon, and the falling of the stars as signs preceding His second coming.

claims.quote: “Then shall the kingdom of heaven be likened unto ten virgins, which took their lamps, and went forth to meet the bridegroom. And five of them were wise, and five were foolish. They that were foolish took their lamps, and took no oil with them: but the wise took oil in their vessels with their lamps. While the bridegroom tarried, they all slumbered and slept. And at midnight there was a cry made, Behold, the bridegroom cometh; go ye out to meet him.”
claims.claim_text: Matthew 25:1-6 contains this text as quoted here in the King James Version.

claims.quote: The coming of Christ, as announced by the first angel’s message, was understood to be represented by the coming of the bridegroom.
claims.claim_text: Adventists interpreted the coming of the bridegroom in the parable as representing Christ's coming as announced by the first angel's message.

claims.quote: The wide-spread reformation under the proclamation of His soon coming, answered to the going forth of the virgins.
claims.claim_text: The proclamation of Christ's soon coming by the Millerite movement produced a wide-spread religious reformation/revival.

claims.quote: By the tarrying of the bridegroom is represented the passing of the time when the Lord was expected, the disappointment, and the seeming delay.
claims.claim_text: The bridegroom's tarrying in the parable is interpreted as representing the passing of 1844 and the Millerite disappointment.

claims.quote: About this time, fanaticism began to appear. Some who had professed to be zealous believers in the message, rejected the word of God as the one infallible guide, and claiming to be led by the Spirit, gave themselves up to the control of their own feelings, impressions, and imaginations.
claims.claim_text: Around the time of the 1844 "tarrying time," fanaticism appeared among some professed Advent believers, with some rejecting sole scriptural authority and claiming direct Spirit-leading of their feelings and impressions.

claims.quote: The people had been greatly stirred by the Advent Movement, thousands of sinners had been converted, and faithful men were giving themselves to the work of proclaiming the truth, even in the tarrying time.
claims.claim_text: Thousands of people were converted through the Advent Movement, including during the 1844 "tarrying time."

claims.quote: Satan was seeking by this means to oppose and destroy the work of God.
claims.claim_text: Satan sought, through fanaticism among Adventists, to oppose and destroy the work of God.

claims.quote: Satan is “the accuser of the brethren,” and it is his spirit that inspires men to watch for the errors and defects of the Lord’s people, and to hold them up to notice, while their good deeds are passed by without a mention.
claims.claim_text: Satan, as "the accuser of the brethren," inspires people to publicize the errors of God's people while ignoring their good deeds.

claims.quote: In all the history of the church, no reformation has been carried forward without encountering serious obstacles.
claims.claim_text: No reformation in church history has been carried forward without encountering serious obstacles.

claims.quote: Thus it was in Paul’s day. Wherever the apostle raised up a church, there were some who professed to receive the faith, but who brought in heresies, that, if received, would eventually crowd out the love of the truth.
claims.claim_text: In the apostle Paul's time, in churches he established, some who professed the faith introduced heresies that threatened to crowd out true doctrine.

claims.quote: Luther also suffered great perplexity and distress from the course of fanatical persons who claimed that God had spoken directly through them, and who therefore set their own ideas and opinions above the testimony of the Scriptures.
claims.claim_text: Martin Luther experienced perplexity and distress due to fanatical persons who claimed direct divine revelation and placed their own opinions above Scripture.

claims.quote: And the Wesleys, and others who blessed the world by their influence and their faith, encountered at every step the wiles of Satan in pushing overzealous, unbalanced, and unsanctified ones into fanaticism of every grade.
claims.claim_text: John and Charles Wesley encountered fanaticism among overzealous, unbalanced followers throughout their ministry.

claims.quote: William Miller had no sympathy with those influences that led to fanaticism.
claims.claim_text: William Miller had no sympathy with the influences that led to fanaticism among Adventists.

claims.quote: He declared, with Luther, that every spirit should be tested by the word of God.
claims.claim_text: William Miller stated that every spirit/claim should be tested by the word of God, echoing Luther's position.

claims.quote: “The devil,” said Miller, “has great power over the minds of some at the present day. And how shall we know what manner of spirit they are of? The Bible answers: ‘By their fruits ye shall know them.’... There are many spirits gone out into the world; and we are commanded to try the spirits. The spirit that does not cause us to live soberly, righteously, and godly, in this present world, is not the Spirit of Christ. I am more and more convinced that Satan has much to do in these wild movements.... Many among us, who pretend to be wholly sanctified, are following the traditions of men, and apparently are as ignorant of truth as others who make no such pretensions.”
claims.claim_text: William Miller is quoted as stating that the devil had great power over the minds of some believers and that Satan had much to do with the "wild movements" of fanaticism.

claims.quote: “The spirit of error will lead us from the truth; and the Spirit of God will lead us into truth. But, say you, a man may be in an error, and think he has the truth. What then? We answer, The Spirit and word agree. If a man judges himself by the word of God, and finds a perfect harmony through the whole word, then he must believe he has the truth; but if he finds the spirit by which he is led does not harmonize with the whole tenor of God’s law or book, then let him walk carefully, lest he be caught in the snare of the devil.”
claims.claim_text: William Miller is quoted as teaching that any spirit must be tested against harmony with the whole of Scripture.

claims.quote: “I have often obtained more evidence of inward piety from a kindling eye, a wet cheek, and a choked utterance, than from all the noise in Christendom.”
claims.claim_text: William Miller is quoted as saying he obtained more evidence of inward piety from quiet emotional signs than from religious "noise."

claims.quote: In the days of the Reformation its enemies charged all the evils of fanaticism upon the very ones who were laboring most earnestly against it.
claims.claim_text: During the Reformation, opponents attributed the evils of fanaticism to the reformers who were themselves working most earnestly against fanaticism.

claims.quote: A similar course was pursued by the opposers of the Advent Movement.
claims.claim_text: Opponents of the Millerite Advent Movement pursued a course similar to Reformation-era enemies, blaming fanaticism on the movement's leaders.

claims.quote: not content with misrepresenting and exaggerating the errors of extremists and fanatics, they circulated unfavorable reports that had not the slightest semblance of truth.
claims.claim_text: Opponents of the Advent Movement circulated false, baseless reports about Adventists in addition to exaggerating actual fanatic behavior.

claims.quote: The fact that a few fanatics worked their way into the ranks of Adventists is no more a reason to decide that the movement was not of God, than was the presence of fanatics and deceivers in the church in Paul’s or Luther’s day a sufficient excuse for condemning their work.
claims.claim_text: The presence of a few fanatics among Adventists is no more grounds for condemning the movement than the presence of fanatics in Paul's or Luther's churches was grounds for condemning their work.

claims.quote: These appeared in the summer of 1844, when Adventists were in a state of doubt and perplexity concerning their real position.
claims.claim_text: Fanaticism and division among Adventists appeared in the summer of 1844, a period of doubt about their prophetic position.

claims.quote: It was not the proclamation of the second advent that created fanaticism and division.
claims.claim_text: The proclamation of the second advent itself was not the cause of fanaticism and division among Adventists.

claims.quote: The preaching of the first angel’s message and of the “midnight cry” tended directly to repress fanaticism and dissension.
claims.claim_text: The preaching of the first angel's message and the "midnight cry" directly tended to repress fanaticism and dissension among Adventists.

claims.quote: “While the bridegroom tarried, they all slumbered and slept. And at midnight there was a cry made, Behold, the bridegroom cometh; go ye out to meet him. Then all those virgins arose, and trimmed their lamps.”
claims.claim_text: Matthew 25:5-7 contains this text as quoted here in the King James Version.

claims.quote: In the summer of 1844, midway between the time when it had been first thought that the 2300 days would end, and the autumn of the same year, to which it was afterward found that they extended, the message was proclaimed in the very words of Scripture, “Behold, the Bridegroom cometh!”
claims.claim_text: The "midnight cry" message, "Behold, the Bridegroom cometh," was proclaimed by Millerites in the summer of 1844.

claims.quote: That which led to this movement was the discovery that the decree of Artaxerxes for the restoration of Jerusalem, which formed the starting-point for the period of the 2300 days, went into effect in the autumn of the year B.C. 457, and not at the beginning of the year, as had been formerly believed.
claims.claim_text: The decree of Artaxerxes for the restoration of Jerusalem, used by Millerites as the starting point of the 2300-day prophecy, went into effect in the autumn of 457 BC rather than at the start of that year.

claims.quote: Reckoning from the autumn of 457, the 2300 years terminate in the autumn of 1844.
claims.claim_text: Millerites calculated that, reckoning from the autumn of 457 BC, the 2300-year prophetic period terminated in the autumn of 1844.

claims.quote: Arguments drawn from the Old Testament types also pointed to the autumn as the time when the event represented by the “cleansing of the sanctuary” must take place.
claims.claim_text: Millerites believed Old Testament typology pointed to autumn as the time for the "cleansing of the sanctuary" to occur.

claims.quote: Says Paul, “Christ our passover is sacrificed for us.”
claims.claim_text: The apostle Paul wrote, "Christ our passover is sacrificed for us" (1 Corinthians 5:7).

claims.quote: Paul says, in speaking of the resurrection of the Lord, and of all His people, “Christ the first-fruits; afterward they that are Christ’s at His coming.”
claims.claim_text: The apostle Paul wrote, "Christ the first-fruits; afterward they that are Christ's at His coming" (1 Corinthians 15:23).

claims.quote: The slaying of the Passover lamb was a shadow of the death of Christ.
claims.claim_text: The Passover lamb's slaying is a typological shadow of Christ's death.

claims.quote: The sheaf of first-fruits, which at the time of the Passover was waved before the Lord, was typical of the resurrection of Christ.
claims.claim_text: The wave-sheaf of first-fruits offered at Passover is typologically representative of Christ's resurrection.

claims.quote: On the fourteenth day of the first Jewish month, the very day and month on which, for fifteen long centuries, the Passover lamb had been slain, Christ, having eaten the Passover with His disciples, instituted that feast which was to commemorate His own death as “the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world.”
claims.claim_text: Christ instituted the Lord's Supper on the fourteenth day of the first Jewish month (Nisan), the same calendar date on which the Passover lamb had traditionally been slain for approximately fifteen centuries.

claims.quote: That same night He was taken by wicked hands, to be crucified and slain.
claims.claim_text: Christ was arrested the same night He instituted the Lord's Supper and was subsequently crucified.

claims.quote: And as the antitype of the wave-sheaf, our Lord was raised from the dead on the third day, “the first-fruits of them that slept,”
claims.claim_text: Christ was raised from the dead on the third day following His crucifixion.

claims.quote: “the first-fruits of them that slept,”
claims.claim_text: 1 Corinthians 15:20 contains the phrase "the first-fruits of them that slept," attributed to Paul.

claims.quote: a sample of all the resurrected just, whose “vile body” shall be changed, and “fashioned like unto His glorious body.”
claims.claim_text: Philippians 3:21 contains the phrases "vile body" and "fashioned like unto His glorious body," attributed to Paul.

claims.quote: Under the Mosaic system, the cleansing of the sanctuary, or the great day of atonement, occurred on the tenth day of the seventh Jewish month,
claims.claim_text: Under the Mosaic law, the Day of Atonement (cleansing of the sanctuary) occurred annually on the tenth day of the seventh Jewish month.

claims.quote: The tenth day of the seventh month, the great day of atonement, the time of the cleansing of the sanctuary, which in the year 1844 fell upon the twenty-second of October, was regarded as the time of the Lord’s coming.
claims.claim_text: In 1844, the tenth day of the seventh Jewish month (the Day of Atonement) fell on October 22, and Millerites regarded this as the expected time of the Lord's coming.

claims.quote: So it was believed that Christ, our great High Priest, would appear to purify the earth by the destruction of sin and sinners, and to bless His waiting people with immortality.
claims.claim_text: Millerites believed Christ, as great High Priest, would appear on the day of atonement's antitype to purify the earth and bless His people with immortality.

claims.quote: the “midnight cry” was heralded by thousands of believers.
claims.claim_text: Thousands of Adventist believers proclaimed the "midnight cry" message in 1844.

claims.quote: Like a tidal wave the movement swept over the land. From city to city, from village to village, and into remote country places it went, until the waiting people of God were fully aroused.
claims.claim_text: The 1844 "midnight cry" Advent movement spread rapidly across cities, villages, and rural areas.

claims.quote: Said Miller, in describing that work: “There is no great expression of joy: that is, as it were, suppressed for a future occasion, when all heaven and earth will rejoice together with joy unspeakable and full of glory. There is no shouting: that, too, is reserved for the shout from heaven. The singers are silent: they are waiting to join the angelic hosts, the choir from heaven.... There is no clashing of sentiments: all are of one heart and of one mind.”
claims.claim_text: William Miller is quoted describing the 1844 midnight-cry movement as marked by suppressed joy, no shouting, silent singers, and unity of sentiment.

claims.quote: Another who participated in the movement testified: “It produced everywhere the most deep searching of heart and humiliation of soul before the God of high heaven. It caused a weaning of affections from the things of this world, a healing of controversies and animosities, a confession of wrongs, a breaking down before God, and penitent, broken-hearted supplications to Him for pardon and acceptance. It caused self-abasement and prostration of soul, such as we never before witnessed. As God by Joel commanded, when the great day of God should be at hand, it produced a rending of hearts and not of garments, and a turning unto the Lord with fasting, and weeping, and mourning. As God said by Zechariah, a spirit of grace and supplication was poured out upon His children; they looked to Him whom they had pierced, there was a great mourning in the land, ... and those who were looking for the Lord afflicted their souls before Him.”
claims.claim_text: An unnamed participant in the 1844 movement is quoted testifying that it produced widespread heart-searching, humiliation, confession, and mourning among believers.
claims.extractor_note: Source text identifies the speaker only as "another who participated in the movement," not by name.

claims.quote: Of all the great religious movements since the days of the apostles, none have been more free from human imperfection and the wiles of Satan than was that of the autumn of 1844.
claims.claim_text: The autumn 1844 Adventist movement was more free from human imperfection and satanic influence than any other great religious movement since the apostles.

claims.quote: Angels were sent from heaven to arouse those who had become discouraged, and prepare them to receive the message.
claims.claim_text: Angels were sent from heaven to arouse discouraged believers and prepare them to receive the midnight-cry message.

claims.quote: Farmers left their crops standing in the fields, mechanics laid down their tools, and with tears and rejoicing went out to give the warning.
claims.claim_text: During the 1844 midnight-cry movement, farmers left crops unharvested in the fields and mechanics left their tools to go proclaim the warning message.

claims.quote: Those who had formerly led in the cause were among the last to join in this movement.
claims.claim_text: Those who had formerly led the Advent cause were among the last to join the 1844 midnight-cry movement.

claims.quote: The churches in general closed their doors against this message, and a large company of those who received it withdrew from their connection.
claims.claim_text: Established churches generally closed their doors to the midnight-cry message, prompting a large number of Adventist believers to withdraw from their church membership.

claims.quote: Upon the occasion of Christ’s triumphal entry into Jerusalem, the people who were assembled from all parts of the land to keep the feast, flocked to the Mount of Olives, and as they joined the throng that were escorting Jesus, they caught the inspiration of the hour, and helped to swell the shout, “Blessed is He that cometh in the name of the Lord!”
claims.claim_text: During Christ's triumphal entry into Jerusalem, people assembled from across the land for the feast flocked to the Mount of Olives and joined the crowd escorting Jesus.

claims.quote: “Blessed is He that cometh in the name of the Lord!”
claims.claim_text: The crowd at Christ's triumphal entry shouted, "Blessed is He that cometh in the name of the Lord" (cf. Matthew 21:9).

claims.quote: In like manner did unbelievers who flocked to the Adventist meetings—some from curiosity, some merely to ridicule—feel the convincing power attending the message, “Behold, the Bridegroom cometh!”
claims.claim_text: Some unbelievers who attended Adventist meetings in 1844 out of curiosity or to ridicule reportedly felt a convincing spiritual power in the "Behold, the Bridegroom cometh" message.

claims.quote: At that time there was faith that brought answers to prayer,—faith that had respect to the recompense of reward.
claims.claim_text: During the 1844 movement, believers had faith that brought answers to prayer.

claims.quote: They often met together in secluded places to commune with God, and the voice of intercession ascended to heaven from the fields and groves.
claims.claim_text: Adventist believers in 1844 often met in secluded outdoor places, such as fields and groves, for prayer.
claims.extractor_note: General characterization of collective devotional practice; borderline specific but presented as historical fact.

claims.quote: But again they were destined to disappointment. The time of expectation passed, and their Saviour did not appear.
claims.claim_text: October 22, 1844 passed without Christ's expected return, producing a second Millerite disappointment.

claims.quote: “They have taken away my Lord, and I know not where they have laid Him.”
claims.claim_text: Mary is recorded as saying, upon finding Christ's tomb empty, "They have taken away my Lord, and I know not where they have laid Him" (John 20:13).

claims.quote: A large class who had professed to believe in the Lord’s soon coming, renounced their faith.
claims.claim_text: After the October 22, 1844 disappointment, a large number of former believers renounced their Adventist faith.

claims.quote: Those who had based their faith upon the opinions of others, and not upon the word of God, were now as ready again to change their views.
claims.claim_text: Adventist believers whose faith rested on others' opinions rather than personal Scripture study readily abandoned their views after the 1844 disappointment.
claims.extractor_note: General characterization; borderline as a discrete checkable fact but presented as historical claim.

claims.quote: they had, to a great extent, withdrawn from the society of those who did not receive the message.
claims.claim_text: Many Millerite believers had largely withdrawn from social contact with non-believers in anticipation of Christ's 1844 coming.

claims.quote: When Jesus rode triumphantly into Jerusalem, His followers believed that He was about to ascend the throne of David, and deliver Israel from her oppressors.
claims.claim_text: At Christ's triumphal entry into Jerusalem, His followers believed He was about to ascend David's throne and deliver Israel from its oppressors.

claims.quote: Many spread their outer garments as a carpet in His path, or strewed before Him the leafy branches of the palm.
claims.claim_text: At Christ's triumphal entry, people spread their outer garments and palm branches in His path.

claims.quote: In their enthusiastic joy they united in the glad acclaim, “Hosanna to the Son of David!”
claims.claim_text: The crowd at Christ's triumphal entry shouted "Hosanna to the Son of David" (Matthew 21:9).

claims.quote: He replied, “If these should hold their peace, the stones would immediately cry out.”
claims.claim_text: Jesus is recorded as replying to the Pharisees, "If these should hold their peace, the stones would immediately cry out" (Luke 19:40).

claims.quote: Not till their Lord had come forth triumphant from the grave could they perceive that all had been foretold by prophecy, and “that Christ must needs have suffered, and risen again from the dead.”
claims.claim_text: Only after Christ's resurrection did His disciples understand that His suffering and rising had been foretold by prophecy.

claims.quote: “Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Zion; shout, O daughter of Jerusalem: behold, thy King cometh unto thee: He is just, and having salvation; lowly, and riding upon an ass, and upon a colt the foal of an ass.”
claims.claim_text: The prophet Zechariah is quoted as declaring this prophecy (Zechariah 9:9) regarding the coming King.

claims.quote: Five hundred years before, the Lord had declared by the prophet Zechariah,
claims.claim_text: The prophet Zechariah delivered his prophecy of the King riding on a colt approximately five hundred years before Christ's triumphal entry into Jerusalem.

claims.quote: In like manner, Miller and his associates fulfilled prophecy, and gave a message which Inspiration had foretold should be given to the world, but which they could not have given had they fully understood the prophecies pointing out their disappointment,
claims.claim_text: William Miller and his associates fulfilled biblical prophecy by giving a divinely foretold message, which they could not have given had they fully understood the prophecies of their own disappointment.

claims.quote: The ablest of their opponents had not succeeded in overthrowing their system of prophetic interpretation.
claims.claim_text: The ablest opponents of the Millerites had not succeeded in overthrowing the Millerite system of prophetic interpretation.

claims.quote: When Jonah proclaimed in the streets of Nineveh that within forty days the city would be overthrown, the Lord accepted the humiliation of the Ninevites, and extended their period of probation;
claims.claim_text: The prophet Jonah proclaimed in the streets of Nineveh that the city would be overthrown within forty days, and the Lord accepted the Ninevites' humiliation and extended their probation.

claims.quote: “It has,” they declared, “tested the hearts of all who heard it, and awakened a love for the Lord’s appearing; or it has called forth a hatred, more or less perceivable, but known to God, of His coming. It has drawn a line, ... so that those who will examine their own hearts, may know on which side of it they would have been found, had the Lord then come—whether they would have exclaimed, ‘Lo! this is our God, we have waited for Him, and He will save us;’ or whether they would have called to the rocks and mountains to fall on them to hide them from the face of Him that sitteth on the throne, and from the wrath of the Lamb. God thus, as we believe, has tested His people, has tried their faith, has proved them, and seen whether they would shrink, in the hour of trial, from the position in which He might see fit to place them; and whether they would relinquish this world and rely with implicit confidence in the word of God.”
claims.claim_text: Adventists are quoted declaring that the 1844 message tested the hearts of all who heard it and drew a line revealing love or hatred of the Lord's appearing.
claims.extractor_note: Attributed collectively to Adventist believers ("they declared"), not a single named individual in the source text.

claims.quote: “Were I to live my life over again, with the same evidence that I then had, to be honest with God and man I should have to do as I have done.”
claims.claim_text: William Miller is quoted as saying that, given the same evidence, he would repeat his actions if he lived his life again.

claims.quote: “I hope that I have cleansed my garments from the blood of souls. I feel that, as far as it was in my power, I have freed myself from all guilt in their condemnation.”
claims.claim_text: William Miller is quoted expressing hope that he had cleansed himself of guilt for others' spiritual condemnation.

claims.quote: “Although I have been twice disappointed,” wrote this man of God,
claims.claim_text: William Miller experienced two disappointments regarding Christ's expected return in 1844 (spring and autumn).

claims.quote: “I am not yet cast down or discouraged.... My hope in the coming of Christ is as strong as ever. I have done only what, after years of solemn consideration, I felt it my solemn duty to do. If I have erred, it has been on the side of charity, love to my fellow-men, and conviction of duty to God.”
claims.claim_text: William Miller is quoted stating that his hope in Christ's coming remained strong despite disappointment, and that any error on his part had been on the side of charity and duty.

claims.quote: “One thing I do know, I have preached nothing but what I believed; and God has been with me; His power has been manifested in the work, and much good has been effected.”
claims.claim_text: William Miller is quoted asserting that he preached only what he believed, that God's power had been manifested in his work, and that much good had resulted.

claims.quote: “Many thousands, to all human appearance, have been made to study the Scriptures by the preaching of the time; and by that means, through faith and the sprinkling of the blood of Christ, have been reconciled to God.”
claims.claim_text: William Miller is quoted claiming that many thousands were led to study the Scriptures because of his preaching and were reconciled to God as a result.

claims.quote: “I have never courted the smiles of the proud, nor quailed when the world frowned. I shall not now purchase their favor, nor shall I go beyond duty to tempt their hate. I shall never seek my life at their hands, nor shrink, I hope, from losing it, if God in His good providence so orders.”
claims.claim_text: William Miller is quoted stating that he had never courted approval from the powerful nor feared the world's disapproval.

claims.quote: “Cast not away therefore your confidence, which hath great recompense of reward. For ye have need of patience, that, after ye have done the will of God, ye might receive the promise. For yet a little while, and He that shall come will come, and will not tarry. Now the just shall live by faith: but if any man draw back, My soul shall have no pleasure in him. But we are not of them who draw back unto perdition; but of them that believe to the saving of the soul.”
claims.claim_text: The Epistle to the Hebrews (10:35-39) contains this text as quoted here in the King James Version.

claims.quote: That this admonition is addressed to the church in the last days is evident from the words pointing to the nearness of the Lord’s coming: “For yet a little while, and He that shall come will come, and will not tarry.”
claims.claim_text: The admonition in Hebrews 10:35-39 is addressed prophetically to the church of the last days, as indicated by its reference to the nearness of the Lord's coming.
